title: Finite Difference: Explicit / Implicit / Crank-Nicolson

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Heat equation u_tau = (1/2) sigma^2 u_xx (BSM PDE under change of variables). Discretize: x_j = x_0 + j * Delta_x for j = 0..J; tau_n = n * Delta_tau. u_j^n approximates u(x_j, tau_n).
rules: Explicit (forward time, central space): u_j^(n+1) = alpha * u_{j-1}^n + (1 - 2 alpha) * u_j^n + alpha * u_{j+1}^n where alpha = (sigma^2/2) * Delta_tau / Delta_x^2.
Stability for explicit: alpha <= 1/2, i.e., Delta_tau / Delta_x^2 <= 1 / sigma^2.
Implicit (backward time, central space): solve a tridiagonal system at each step. Unconditionally stable.
Crank-Nicolson: average of explicit and implicit. Stable + second-order accurate in BOTH time and space.
setup.question: Compare the three schemes on stability, accuracy, and per-step cost.

learningObjectives: Write the explicit update formula and identify the stability condition.
Recognize implicit requires a tridiagonal solve per step.
Identify Crank-Nicolson as the practical workhorse: O((Delta_tau)^2, (Delta_x)^2) accuracy and unconditionally stable.

stages.label: Explicit stability
stages.prompt: Explicit FD update: u_j^(n+1) = alpha * u_{j-1}^n + (1 - 2 alpha) * u_j^n + alpha * u_{j+1}^n. Stability requires?

options.text: alpha = (sigma^2 / 2) * Delta_tau / Delta_x^2 <= 1/2 (so the middle coefficient (1 - 2 alpha) is non-negative).  Else: noise amplifies and the scheme blows up.
alpha can be any positive number.

stages.explanation: Von Neumann stability: substitute u_j^n = g^n exp(i k j Delta_x) and require |g| <= 1 for all k. Yields the alpha <= 1/2 bound. If alpha > 1/2, high-frequency modes grow unboundedly.

stages.label: Implicit method
stages.prompt: Implicit FD: u_j^(n+1) related to u_j^n via central differences at TIME n+1. Each time step requires solving what?

options.text: A tridiagonal linear system A * u^(n+1) = u^n where A is constant in n. O(J) per step using the Thomas algorithm.
A dense linear system. O(J^3) per step.

stages.explanation: Backward time difference yields u_j^(n+1) - alpha (u_{j-1}^(n+1) - 2 u_j^(n+1) + u_{j+1}^(n+1)) = u_j^n. The matrix is tridiagonal -- Thomas algorithm solves in O(J). Unconditionally stable: any Delta_tau, Delta_x work.

stages.label: Crank-Nicolson advantage
stages.prompt: Crank-Nicolson averages explicit and implicit RHS. Why is it preferred?

options.text: Unconditionally stable AND second-order accurate in both Delta_tau and Delta_x. Better accuracy/cost than either alone.
It's faster than explicit per step.

stages.explanation: Explicit is O(Delta_tau, Delta_x^2). Implicit is the same. Crank-Nicolson is O((Delta_tau)^2, (Delta_x)^2) -- one extra order in time. Same per-step cost as implicit (tridiagonal solve). Modest oscillations near payoff kinks may require Rannacher smoothing.

stages.label: Commit -- compare schemes
stages.template: Explicit: __[a]__ stable. Implicit: __[b]__ stable. Crank-Nicolson: unconditionally stable + __[c]__-order accurate in time. Per-step cost for implicit / CN: O(__[d]__) tridiagonal solve.
stages.explanation: Memorize this table for rate-curve / option pricing interviews. Crank-Nicolson is the go-to in production for European-style options on a single underlying.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is too many SPACE steps WORSE than too many time steps for explicit FD? (alpha = Delta_tau / Delta_x^2: shrinking Delta_x squares the denominator, so alpha grows quadratically -- easier to violate stability.)
How would you handle American options with FD? (Apply max(continuation value, intrinsic value) at each grid node after each backward time step -- projected SOR or LCP solver.)
Compare to multinomial trees (binomial, trinomial). (Trees are explicit FD in disguise; same stability constraints.)
